dialogue.utterance: Unit 34 to [CITY] regarding G2 P1 patient in preterm labor, gestational sac intact.
I'm Cara with Rescue. Can you tell me your name and what brought you to call 911?
I think I'm delivering. The sac came out and the baby didn't move.
Are you awake, and can you respond to questions?
Yes, I'm Sophia. Can you hold my hand during the next contraction?
What's the current year?
2020. I do need to push but not sure how.
Your airway is clear and patent. Is it difficult to talk through a contraction?
Your breaths are 18 per minute, normal on both sides with no cyanosis or distress noted.
No work of breathing observed. Lungs sound clear bilaterally.
Checking your pulse and pressure now.Peripheral pulses are strong and palpable.
Pulse is 81, strong and regular with a normal rhythm.
Respiratory rate at 18. Now checking your blood pressure.
Blood pressure is 142 over 83 mmHg, stable and within normal range for pregnancy.
Oxygen saturation is 99%, all good here with no desaturation observed on room air.
Glucose is 116 mg/dL. Now starting the EKG monitoring in four lead configuration per protocol.
EKG reading shows sinus rhythm and no arrhythmias present at this time.
Tell me about the events before calling 911 and your symptoms in detail.
Contractions started around 6:30, and a bit of emesis. I then felt the sac come out with no pain in the sac delivery itself. The baby hasn't moved since.
On a scale of 0-10, how severe is your pain right now at this moment?
It's a 6. The pain began about early morning and has continued intermittently with contractions.
What seems to make the pain better or worse during contractions?
It's a sharp, cramping pain in my lower belly and back. It's cramping and not throbbing at all.
Is the pain staying in your lower abdomen or does it go anywhere else such as your legs or chest?
It's continuous, not stopping but is cyclical with contractions about 2 mins apart and 30 seconds long. It hasn't improved at all with rest or position change.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: Demonstrate a sound for us to check your airway and swallowing function.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: Ah-* and no fluid or drainage present in the mouth, nose or ears upon inspection.
Any fluid or discharge from nose or ears?
No, everything else is normal in that area. Vision and extraocular movement seem okay as well.
You're alert, oriented to self, place, time, and person. No signs of neck rigidity or pain found during inspection or palpation of the cervical area.

I just placed an 18 gauge IV lock in your left arm for possible access if needed during transport or in the triage process. It is secured and functioning well per protocol for OB/Gyn transported patients with intact gestational sacs.
Please let me know if the IV site causes you any discomfort or if you feel lightheaded at all due to fluctuating fluid dynamics or position changes.
Following OB/Gyn - Childbirth/Labor/Delivery Protocol 6-2 now to maintain assessment and manage intervention for preterm delivery with intact gestational sac.
Glucose is at 0 currently. Have you taken anything orally since early this morning?
IV in the left arm remains patent and intact. No bleeding or swelling noted, and it shows good flow characteristics on recheck.
Confirming no food or fluid intake since early in the morning, glucose maintenance will be closely observed en route.
EKG is still showing normal sinus rhythm with no significant changes to the cardiac rhythm or electrical conduction evident.
Do you have any known allergies or medications you take that would affect the labor or transport process?
No known drug allergies and not taking any medication aside from prenatal vitamins as reported to prior prenatal care.
Have you had prenatal care for this pregnancy? If so, was everything consistent in your check-ups?
Yes, had regular prenatal appointments. G2 P1, everything was uneventful. I was low-risk but discovered it's a preterm sac delivery anyway when it came out on its own.
What did you have for food or drink this morning before the contractions started?
Just a little water, very minimal. I wasn't eating much during the morning due to cramping and nausea.
We're heading to [COMPANY_NAME] for specialized care. Any specific hospital preferences you've had discussed by providers from previous births?
I'll be placing you on the stretcher now. Have you ever had issues with lying flat for extended periods or transport?
You're cleared and secured on the stretcher. The gestational sac remains intact, and we preserved your position as per patient request during your initial assessment.
Another contraction-how intense is it this time compared to your initial report?
A bit stronger. Maybe an 8 now-this one's longer and feels more consistent with pushing.
L&D staff have been notified with specific details about the intact sac and child status during transport.
All monitoring in place, and you are secured for the transport. We must remain poised for any further manifestation of labor during this movement.
Pulse is currently at 91, stable but slightly increased during contractions. No dysrhythmias seen in the EKG readings during monitoring.
Blood pressure remains 135/71, no major fluctuations noted with movements or pain intensity in this preterm labor progression.
Oxygen saturation is now back to 100% on room air with no signs of desaturation since the previous read.
EKG remains normal, and we are monitoring all other devices in real-time for consistent support during this transport calibration.
Are your contractions still about two minutes apart and maintaining that same lasting time of 30 seconds? Let me know if you need any adjustment or support.
Yes, about 2 minutes apart and 30 seconds lasting. I don't feel like it's progressing at the moment since the sac is intact.
Your last birth was a normal vaginal delivery without complications. Can you confirm that for your new cord vs. your previous singleton intake?
Yes, the first time and also had a normal delivery with no tears or episiotomies.
You said you've had just a little water this morning. Correct?
Yes, only small amounts of water since the early morning.
All monitoring devices including IV and EKG point to stability-no immediate issues during reassessment.
Everything is in place for transport. Let me know if the labor contractions continue to intensify.
Confirmed gestational sac remains intact since arrival, with no physical movement of the sac contents toward delivery.
IV remains secure and unobstructed in left arm. No rechecking needed unless patient feels lightheaded or needs emergency fluids.
Has the pain increased in your abdomen with the last progression?
Slight increase, nothing major-more rhythmic but consistent with prior baseline.
IV and sac all secure-remind me again if you feel something coming out of the sac or displace during travel.
No changes noted on EKG and IV line; system remains within protocol for dynamic monitoring transport.
Glucose is still at 0, and we're keeping an eye for any potential drops associated with prolonged preterm labor.
Blood pressure is stable at 135/71 with no change during reassessment. Heart rate remains strong despite contraction activity.
You are secured and en route to specialized care for this OB/Gyn event. Any physical movement or worsening felt?

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Normal delivery with no complications before, correct?

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Yes, first and second delivery were both smooth with provider support in full.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: No new findings during transport-continues matching initial assessment from arrival. Complete monitoring was engaged and proven stable en route.
Patient en route to [COMPANY_NAME] for L&D evaluation and definitive care. Move is coordinated for a direct handoff for hospital delivery.
Gestational sac remains intact and viable for hospital-based delivery. L&D staff aware from repeated EMD updates.
Patient is secured and pregnant in active preterm labor with intact sac. Proactive measures for spontaneous rupture or emergency delivery have been planned for transition.
Bedside report and verbal report from both EMT and Partner will be given at the hospital transports receiving entrance to coordinate handling of patient and OB event.
Hospital team delivered the baby on the cot in the emergency due to spontaneous delivery following arrival and found to be stillborn as per the patient's initial report here.
Arrived at [COMPANY_NAME] for OB care. The patient's bed is prepared and conception transfer awaiting for immediate adjudicated handover.
All protocols followed, including IV lock and monitoring systems, and the sac remains stable for handover.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Past OB history remains the same as on arrival and during transport: normal past delivery with no reported complications in either term childbirths.
We will provide bedside report for stable vital signs and a G2 P1 patient with intact OB sac. L&D is briefed over our existing findings from field evaluation to hospital room.

EKG and IV particularly remain stable during the entire movement. No lapses observed with another deliver already handled under this stillborn response provided directly by emergency cot delivery before transport procedurally.
IV lock remains intact and unopened since placement at the start of protocol, with all assessments confirming appropriate locking functionality and no device mobility since initial securing.
The patient was a G2 P1 individual during transport with a safeguarded 18 gauge IV lock placed in the left arm, functional for direct hospital use with no obstruction to real-time hospital care.
No complication during entire transport-patient maintained a AVPU score of 4/4. No signs of mental confusion, neck rigidity, or focal deficit were observed in addition to initial findings.
Everything remains consistent with A&O x4-patient is oriented to time, place, person, and self throughout transport without deviation or acutely progressive event threatening her or the fetus.
IV remains secure and part of the hospital handoff, as does our detailed transport report with explicit gestational sac and patient information for timely discharge or follow-up measures.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: No prior surgical history or maternal complications impressed. All information from the provider aligns with this G2 P1 report as low-risk until this spontaneous preterm delivery phase.

dialogue.micro_intent: reassessments - retake history - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Relevant OB history remains currently stable, with L&D team ready to take over for possible surgical management or now backfield reporting while coordinating emergency unit.
IV lock remains unopened but ready for direct hospital access in compliance with transfer protocol between primary and tertiary care delivery for continuity of care and clinical decision-making.
The IV was properly locked at the end of EMT rigging phase and remains in place as an existing access point with no medical barrier noted for hospital initiative intervention.
Complete handover with IV lock intact, OB sac preserved for delivery once placed into L&D care. Blood pressure as last recorded was 135/71 without abnormal events.

All action items completed. Patient and her event has already been delivered by hospital emergency team upon arrival, ensuring safe and emotional support during emergency patient delivery before full hospital integration.
All core maternal and fetal data preserved and provided directly to hospital staff. We covered all baseline indication and transport event accordingly within field report and ambulance care regimes.

Patient now under L&D care team and is being processed per hospital protocol. We thank the team and prepare for any further follow-up with this patient once a safe transfer is completed per hospital agreement.

This concludes field EMS treatment in OB/Gyn delivery scenario with preterm sac intact delivery. The escort of patient to care and emergency baby handling is now officially passed during transport segment to emergency departments upon arrival.
Transport secured and exiting ambulance. All in tandem with L&D intake and surveillance for any possible maternal or neonatal emergency beyond our dispatched and field event.

[COMPANY_NAME] has received and acknowledged the care for further handling as per assessment and field report. Ambush of transportation completed safely with no bouncing from core patient relay period at hospital received door entry.
